# **Production-Grade Controllable Image Synthesis: Aligning Probabilistic Diffusion with Deterministic CAD Pipelines via Google Imagen**

The integration of generative artificial intelligence into deterministic Computer-Aided Design (CAD) and OpenGL rendering pipelines presents a fundamental architectural challenge: reconciling the rigid, mathematical precision of multi-dimensional view matrices with the probabilistic, latent-space hallucinations of diffusion models. The migration of modern local G-Buffer frameworks, such as "Alice Viewer 2," to cloud-native neural rendering pipelines utilizing the Google Imagen API (via Vertex AI) demands a strategy that enforces absolute geometric adherence.1 When generating photorealistic architectural visualizations, structural deviations, silhouette hallucinations, and boundary erosion are unacceptable. The generative trilemma—balancing quality, speed, and diversity—is further complicated by the necessity for absolute spatial control.2

This research report architects an exhaustive, production-grade strategy for deploying the Google Imagen API as a strict neural renderer. It investigates undocumented API payload configurations, formulates mathematical mappings between OpenGL spatial data and diffusion tensor conditions, evaluates advanced mechanisms for decoupling silhouette preservation from texture synthesis using attention masking, and designs a resilient hybrid fallback workflow utilizing deterministic G-buffer compositing.

## **API-Native Structural Conditioning: Exploiting the Vertex AI Architecture**

Standard text-to-image diffusion models are optimized for semantic fidelity and aesthetic appeal, often at the expense of strict spatial consistency.3 To enforce CAD-level precision, the generative pipeline must leverage specialized endpoints designed for structural conditioning. The Google Vertex AI platform differentiates its inference engines, exposing its most advanced image editing and structural conditioning capabilities through the imagen-3.0-capability-001 endpoint, while standard text-to-image generation is routed through imagen-3.0-generate-002.4 For a CAD application demanding geometric adherence, the capability-001 model must be exclusively utilized, as it natively exposes ControlNet-equivalent adapters and advanced localized masking configurations necessary for stencil adherence.5

### **Instantiating ControlNet-Equivalent Adapters via API Payloads**

The Imagen 3 Capability endpoint supports explicit structural conditioning via the ControlNetConfig and ControlNetConditionConfig objects.7 Within this framework, the API natively supports conditioning algorithms highly relevant for CAD stencils, specifically exposed through the conditionName enumerator which accepts cannyEdges and depth.8 While raw semantic segmentation adapters are abstracted from direct user access in the conditionName enumerator, explicit segmentation adherence can be forced using a mathematically rigorous application of MaskImageConfig.9

To treat local G-Buffer passes as absolute physical constraints, the API payload must be structured to bypass the model's default creative thresholds. The following parameters dictate structural authority within the ControlNetConditionConfig object:

| Parameter | Type | Validation Range | Architectural Function |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| conditionMapBytesBase64Encoded | String (Base64) | N/A | Bypasses Vertex AI's internal preprocessors (which are tuned for natural photography, not sharp CAD edges). By providing the raw bytes of the locally generated OpenGL depth buffer or Canny edge detection, the request forces the neural network to ingest the deterministic stencil directly.8 |
| conditionWeight | Float | ![][image1] | Scales the influence of the ControlNet adapters on the U-Net feature maps. For strict CAD geometry, this must be aggressively clamped to 1.0 (maximum authority). Lower values allow the diffusion process to drift from the input geometry.8 |
| conditionMaxT | Float | ![][image1] | Dictates the diffusion timestep ![][image2] at which the ControlNet stops injecting structural priors.8 Setting this to 1.0 forces structural adherence throughout the entire reverse diffusion process. |

Optimal CAD configurations typically utilize a conditionMaxT of 0.85 to 0.95. During the forward diffusion process, the model learns a Markov chain of latent variables ![][image3].10 The reverse process ![][image4] denoises the image.11 If conditionMaxT is set to 1.0, the structural condition governs every step, which often results in "baked-in" artifacts where flat CAD surfaces look unnaturally uniform and lack high-frequency photorealistic noise. Dropping the condition at 0.95 allows the final 5% of high-frequency denoising steps to resolve natural textures—such as the micro-variations in concrete or the specular highlights on glass—without destroying the underlying low-frequency edge geometry established in the earlier steps.

![][image5]

### **The editConfig Override: Forcing Sub-Pixel Mask Adherence**

When utilizing segmentation masks to constrain the generation to specific architectural elements (for instance, replacing only a brutalist facade while leaving the sky and foreground foliage intact), standard API endpoints often apply automated dilation to masks to softly blend the newly generated pixels with the unmasked region.12 In a strict CAD environment, this automated feathering ruins the mathematically derived silhouette.

The Vertex AI editConfig schema exposes advanced parameters that allow engineers to bypass these consumer-friendly blending features, forcing the neural network to respect the exact boolean logic of the segmentation mask:

* **enable\_clamping**: When set to true, this boolean bypasses the model's internal mask dilation, allowing the client to fully control the boundary of the edit.7 This ensures the neural renderer does not extend its generation a single pixel beyond the provided segmentation stencil, preserving unmasked areas perfectly.  
* **mask\_dilation**: Even with clamping enabled, this parameter must be explicitly overridden to 0.0.13 The default value of 0.03 represents a percentage of the image width to grow the mask by, which would introduce massive structural deviations in a high-resolution render.  
* **base\_guidance\_scale**: The standard text-to-image guidance scale enforces adherence to the text prompt via classifier-free guidance, but high values often degrade structural geometry as the model prioritizes semantic concepts over the conditioning image.7 The editConfig allows passing an array of up to 4 integers representing the guidance scale during different stages of diffusion (from fine-grained to coarse).7 For CAD alignment, configuring this array to heavily prioritize text guidance during the initial, coarse stages (where macro-structure and layout are formed) and dropping guidance to zero during the fine-grained stages allows the model to "settle" into the exact edges dictated by the ControlNet without conflicting textual distortions dragging the pixels out of alignment.

By combining enable\_clamping \= true with a raw conditionMapBytesBase64Encoded stencil, the probabilistic nature of the diffusion model is tightly corralled into a deterministic spatial boundary.

## **Mathematical Camera Alignment: Bridging the M4 View Matrix to Diffusion Tensors**

Textual prompts are fundamentally inadequate for enforcing exact field of view (FOV), pitch, yaw, and focal length.10 A prompt detailing a "50mm lens, wide angle, low poly, looking up" provides only semantic weighting in the latent space; it does not explicitly alter the frustum geometry of the resulting image.16 Natural language cannot encapsulate the trigonometric ratios required to establish a precise vanishing point. Therefore, the perspective projection matrix ![][image6] and the view matrix ![][image7] calculated inside the local "Alice Viewer 2" environment must be mathematically encoded directly into the spatial conditioning tensors before they are dispatched to the Imagen API.18

### **Translating OpenGL Projection to Metric Depth**

The native depth buffer in a standard OpenGL or DirectX pipeline does not correlate linearly with physical space. To preserve Z-buffer precision near the camera and prevent z-fighting, graphics pipelines apply a hyperbolic transformation based on the near (![][image8]) and far (![][image9]) clipping planes.19

In a standard 4x4 OpenGL projection matrix ![][image6], the third row is responsible for calculating the clip-space Z coordinate. After perspective division by the W-component (![][image10]), the normalized device coordinates (NDC) depth ![][image11] is generated via the following non-linear relationship:

![][image12]  
However, diffusion models conditioned on depth—such as those trained using MiDaS or ZoeDepth architectures—expect relative or metric linear depth.20 If the raw, hyperbolic OpenGL depth buffer is passed directly to the depth ControlNet condition, the neural renderer will perceive a highly distorted, warped spatial volume where objects in the mid-ground appear exponentially closer to the background. This fundamental mismatch causes the diffusion model to hallucinate incorrect perspective foreshortening.

To correct this spatial discrepancy, the local C++ pipeline must intercept the depth pass and invert the hyperbolic mapping to retrieve the linear eye-space depth ![][image13] 19:

![][image14]  
Once linear metric depth is retrieved across the buffer, it must be normalized into a format suitable for the API payload, which typically expects 8-bit or 16-bit integer channels.22 Because the Vertex API decodes base64 strings into standard 8-bit per channel tensors by default, attempting to map a massive physical space (e.g., an architectural campus spanning 500 meters) into 256 discrete integer values will introduce severe quantization errors, resulting in visible "stair-stepping" or banding artifacts in the depth map.23

To mitigate this, min-max normalization must be dynamically applied based strictly on the bounds of the visible geometry, rather than the absolute far clipping plane. If the furthest building in the CAD scene is at 150 meters, normalizing ![][image13] between the nearest fragment and 150 meters maximizes the bit-depth resolution, preventing the diffusion model from interpreting quantized depth bands as physical steps in the geometry.22

![][image15]

### **Frustum Encoding and Implicit FOV Mapping**

The diffusion model must implicitly inherit the camera's perspective matrix. To encode the camera's FOV without a dedicated API parameter, the depth map must be generated using the exact frustum bounds. As established in projection geometry, the focal length in pixel units ![][image16] is directly related to the horizontal field of view (![][image17]) and the sensor width ![][image18] 25:

![][image19]  
By rendering the linear depth map from the exact origin and orientation dictated by the camera's extrinsic parameters (Rotation matrix ![][image20] and Translation vector ![][image21]), the two-dimensional depth map intrinsically carries the 3D geometric relationships of the scene.25 The scale and divergence of the depth values implicitly encode the perspective distortion. When this structurally perfect depth map is supplied to the Imagen depth condition with conditionWeight \= 1.0, the diffusion model lacks the mathematical degrees of freedom to alter the perspective. It is forced to construct the scene adhering to the provided vanishing points.

### **Advanced Implicit Conditioning via Surface Normals**

While linear depth maps provide a scalar distance from the camera, they lack explicit vector directionality. This occasionally confuses diffusion models at sharp, occluding edges, where a foreground plane and a background plane are adjacent in screen space but vastly separated in world space.

An advanced architectural approach to implicitly force the diffusion model to inherit the exact view matrix is by conditioning on Surface Normals, which represent the continuous geometric orientation of the scene.28 While standard API endpoints may abstract native normal map adapters, high-fidelity normal data can often be injected through customized image-to-image inputs where the underlying geometry dictates lighting calculations inside the generative process.

By calculating the cross product of screen-space partial derivatives of the linear depth buffer, the local CAD pipeline can generate a pristine tangent-space or world-space Normal Map in real-time.29 Let ![][image22] be the 3D world-space coordinate reconstructed from the depth map at pixel ![][image23]. The normal vector ![][image24] is derived via the cross product of the partial derivatives with respect to screen coordinates:

![][image25]  
Because the Normal Map explicitly encodes the orientation of every polygon relative to the camera's ray direction, it injects the camera's extrinsic parameters (yaw, pitch, roll) directly into the conditioning tensor.28 Recent literature on camera-conditioned diffusion models explores techniques such as Plücker-ray embeddings, where coordinates encode the ray direction using the camera's intrinsic and extrinsic parameters.10 Providing a normal map effectively mimics this embedding process at the pixel level, giving the U-Net an unambiguous blueprint of the camera's orientation relative to the architectural surfaces.

## **Silhouette Preservation and Texture Noise Decoupling**

A pervasive phenomenon when applying structural controls (like Canny edges or Depth) to diffusion models is the conflation of texture with structure.3 In architectural contexts, a CAD model might define a perfectly smooth vertical plane representing a wall. If the text prompt specifies "brutalist ribbed concrete," the diffusion model attempts to hallucinate high-frequency vertical ridges to satisfy the semantic requirement. However, because the depth map dictates a perfectly flat surface, the model experiences a fundamental clash between the structural prior (flat) and the semantic prior (ribbed). This conflict typically results in either "smoothed out" textures that fail to resemble the prompt, or hallucinatory artifacts bleeding beyond the physical boundary of the wall into adjacent geometry.32

The primary objective is to configure the generation so it strictly obeys the *boundary silhouettes* (the occluding contours) of the input stencils, while allowing the latent space total freedom to hallucinate internal high-frequency textures, lighting, and bump-mapping within those defined boundaries.

### **The Mechanism of Attention Bleed and Hallucinations**

Diffusion models, particularly Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs), are prone to generating structural inconsistencies that lie outside the support of the true data distribution—a phenomenon commonly referred to as hallucination.3 These hallucinations are primarily driven by the internal attention mechanisms. During inference, cross-attention layers distribute textual features across the spatial map, while self-attention layers blend neighboring pixel latents to ensure global coherence.33

Because self-attention operations calculate the dot product between queries and keys across the entire spatial dimension, tokens representing the "sky" can attend to tokens representing the "building." This global blending is responsible for texture bleed; the neural network smooths the transition between two highly contrasting semantic regions, causing the sharp CAD roofline to erode and merge with the background clouds.33

To counteract this, academic literature identifies "Inside-Outside Attention" as the premier mechanism for decoupling silhouette preservation from internal texture synthesis.33 By restricting the U-Net's self-attention mechanism to operate exclusively within the bounds of the segmentation mask, high-frequency textures generated inside the architectural geometry are physically prevented from bleeding into the background. Inside-Outside Attention solves boundary erosion by utilizing a segmentation mask ![][image26] as a hard, binary constraint within the U-Net. During the forward and reverse passes, self-attention operations are segregated:

![][image27]  
![][image28]  
This mathematical segregation ensures that the query vectors ![][image29] inside the building mask ![][image26] evaluate to zero when multiplied by key vectors ![][image30] in the background ![][image31]. Consequently, no information from the background ("outside") can influence the object ("inside"), flawlessly preserving the boundary silhouette defined by the CAD engine.33

### **Emulating Attention Segregation via the API**

Because the Google Imagen API is a fully managed endpoint, developers cannot directly inject custom PyTorch attention layers into the cloud-hosted U-Net.35 However, this theoretical framework of attention segregation can be highly approximated using the API's masking and editConfig capabilities in a specific sequence.

To achieve localized texture freedom while preserving the macro-silhouette, the pipeline must implement a multi-pass conditioning strategy:

1. **Semantic Mask Extraction:** Extract the binary segmentation mask from the CAD software, isolating the target architectural elements from the environment.  
2. **Exterior Canny Filtering:** Extract the Canny edge map from the pure geometry. Critically, apply a localized filter to the Canny map so it *only* includes the exterior silhouettes of the objects. Explicitly remove any internal wireframe lines, coplanar polygon edges, or window mullions from the Canny map.  
3. **Targeted Payload Assembly:** Pass the exterior-only Canny map to the ControlNetConditionConfig (with conditionWeight \= 1.0) and pass the binary segmentation mask into the MaskImageConfig.  
4. **Semantic Filter Enforcement:** The API exposes SemanticFilterConfig. Ensure this is configured to rigidly enforce the bounding box geometries defined by the mask, preventing the model from expanding the semantic concept beyond the masked area.7

By providing a ControlNet condition that strictly defines the exterior boundary, omitting internal structural lines, and locking the generation region with an un-dilated mask (enable\_clamping \= true, dilation \= 0.0), the model is mathematically confined. It is forced to hallucinate the internal textures prompted by the user strictly within the boundaries, fulfilling the requirement of zero silhouette erosion while allowing internal creative freedom.7

### **Structure Preservation Loss (SPL) and Post-Generation Validation**

To further refine the texture-structure decoupling, recent algorithmic advancements highlight the utility of Structure Preservation Loss (SPL).37 SPL demonstrates that utilizing local linear models to quantify structural differences between the input stencils and the edited images drastically improves pixel-level edge retention.37 While SPL is designed to be integrated directly into the diffusion model's generative process, its principles can be adapted for production environments interacting with closed APIs via post-processing validation.

By enacting a Rejection Sampling pipeline, the system can automatically filter out hallucinations. Once the API returns a generated image, a rapid structural similarity index measure (SSIM) or a secondary edge-detection pass is executed over the generated output. The resulting structural map is compared against the original deterministic CAD stencil. Instances that deviate beyond a specified sub-pixel tolerance are automatically discarded, ensuring only geometrically accurate textures are passed down the pipeline.38 This technique shifts the output into a higher-quality region of the model's data space without requiring underlying model retraining.

## **Hybrid Workflows: Deterministic G-Buffer Compositing**

Despite meticulously tuned API payloads, rigorous depth normalization, and rejection sampling, probabilistic diffusion models operating within a closed-source API cannot mathematically guarantee 100% adherence to sub-pixel architectural CAD tolerances in every single generation. The fundamental nature of adding and removing Gaussian noise introduces inherent variance. To achieve a production-grade "zero structural deviation" pipeline, the neural rendering step must be treated as an intermediate texturing and global illumination pass, rather than the final display buffer.

Drawing inspiration from architectures like "DiffusionRenderer"—which utilizes video diffusion model priors to estimate G-buffers and render photorealistic images without explicit light transport simulation 39—we architect a resilient hybrid fallback workflow. This pipeline uses Imagen 3 for base texture and lighting generation, followed by local deterministic masking and depth-compositing within the C++ environment to surgically re-enforce the original geometry.

### **The Deferred Neural Rendering Architecture**

In a standard Deferred Rendering pipeline, geometric data is decoupled from lighting calculations via a G-Buffer. For this hybrid approach, the G-Buffer is repurposed to govern the AI composite, acting as the absolute ground truth.

| Pipeline Stage | Environment | Operations Performed | Output Generated |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **1\. Pass Generation** | Local C++ (Alice Viewer 2\) | Renders a suite of pixel-perfect, aliased passes based on the ![][image7] and ![][image6] matrices. | Albedo/Color ID Pass, Linear Depth Pass (![][image13]), Sobel/Canny Edge Pass, Binary Alpha Segmentation Pass. |
| **2\. Neural Generation** | Cloud (Vertex AI Imagen) | Dispatches the normalized Depth and Canny passes alongside the text prompt (e.g., "Photorealistic rendering, brutalist concrete"). Strict editConfig parameters are applied. | AI-generated Beauty Pass (![][image32]) containing photorealistic textures, global illumination, and specular highlights. |
| **3\. Deterministic Compositing** | Local C++ (Alice Viewer 2\) | Downloads the generated image. Executes pixel-math shaders to multiply the AI beauty pass against the original, flawless OpenGL stencils. | Final Composite (![][image33]) exhibiting photorealistic rendering with zero geometric deviation. |

### **Re-enforcing Geometric Edges Post-Generation**

The core mechanism of the hybrid workflow is the re-multiplication of the AI-generated beauty pass against the original OpenGL stencils. Because the diffusion model may have probabilistically blurred a sharp 90-degree corner by a fraction of a pixel, or hallucinogenically morphed a straight window frame by 2 pixels, the local engine enforces absolute correction.

Let the AI-generated image be ![][image32], the original precise CAD Alpha mask be ![][image34] (where object \= 1, background \= 0), and a locally rendered high-resolution skybox or environment map be ![][image35]. The final composite ![][image33] is computed in the fragment shader via linear interpolation:

![][image36]  
This mathematical multiplication guarantees that regardless of how the diffusion model interpreted the prompt, no pixel representing the building can physically exist outside the strict bounds of ![][image34]. The boundary silhouette is mathematically preserved, eliminating any possibility of background texture bleed.

### **Depth-Tested Rejection Sampling for Internal Geometry**

While Alpha compositing fixes the exterior silhouette, internal edges (such as overlapping balconies, deep window recesses, or structural columns) require a depth-compositing approach. By maintaining the original ![][image13] buffer, any AI-hallucinated geometry that drastically contradicts the actual CAD depth—for example, an AI-generated foreground tree branch probabilistically overlapping a background wall—can be identified and discarded.

This is achieved by applying a localized depth-test threshold. The local pipeline runs a lightweight monocular depth estimation algorithm over the AI-generated image ![][image32] to approximate the perceived depth of the hallucinated textures. By computing the absolute disparity between the AI-perceived structural edges and the deterministic OpenGL G-buffer edges, anomalous pixels are flagged.

![][image37]  
If ![][image38] exceeds a predefined structural tolerance ![][image39], the pixel is rejected and re-sampled from the baseline material colors or re-routed for localized inpainting. This depth-tested rejection ensures that the spatial hierarchy of the scene remains intact.

This hybrid approach ensures that the immense generative capability of the Google Imagen API is fully harnessed for complex ambient occlusion, global illumination simulation, and intricate material texturing, while the strict structural integrity mandated by architectural and engineering specifications remains entirely uncompromised by probabilistic variance.

## **Conclusion**

Migrating deterministic CAD visualization pipelines to probabilistic neural renderers using the Google Imagen API requires systematically overriding the model's inherent propensity for semantic freedom. By capitalizing on the advanced endpoints of Vertex AI—specifically leveraging the imagen-3.0-capability-001 model, disabling internal mask dilations via enable\_clamping, and precisely staging base\_guidance\_scale and conditionMaxT within the editConfig payload—developers can enforce rigorous structural discipline.

Furthermore, true spatial alignment necessitates that local OpenGL projection matrices are mathematically inverted from hyperbolic representations into linear, normalized metric depth tensors prior to API dispatch. While payload configurations analogous to Inside-Outside attention mitigate internal texture bleed at the inference level, the definitive safeguard for production-grade architectural rendering remains a hybrid workflow. By subordinating the diffusion model's output to a final deterministic compositing pass utilizing the original geometric G-buffers, zero structural deviation is reliably and consistently achieved.

33. Shape-Guided Diffusion with Inside-Outside Attention \- arXiv, accessed on April 12, 2026, [https://arxiv.org/html/2212.00210v3](https://arxiv.org/html/2212.00210v3)  
34. Vision and Language Understanding Through Generative Modeling \- EECS, accessed on April 12, 2026, [https://www2.eecs.berkeley.edu/Pubs/TechRpts/2023/EECS-2023-202.pdf](https://www2.eecs.berkeley.edu/Pubs/TechRpts/2023/EECS-2023-202.pdf)  
35. How to Implement Image Generation with Imagen on Vertex AI \- OneUptime, accessed on April 12, 2026, [https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-02-17-how-to-implement-image-generation-with-imagen-on-vertex-ai/view](https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-02-17-how-to-implement-image-generation-with-imagen-on-vertex-ai/view)  
36. API Reference — google\_api\_ai\_platform v0.27.0 \- Log in, accessed on April 12, 2026, [https://hexdocs.pm/google\_api\_ai\_platform/](https://hexdocs.pm/google_api_ai_platform/)  
37. Edge-Aware Image Manipulation via Diffusion Models with a Novel Structure-Preservation Loss \- arXiv, accessed on April 12, 2026, [https://arxiv.org/html/2601.16645v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2601.16645v1)  
38. Stop Your Diffusion Models From Hallucinating: A Simple Trick to Get Way Better Image Quality \- Reddit, accessed on April 12, 2026, [https://www.reddit.com/r/PromptEngineering/comments/1p6krn8/stop\_your\_diffusion\_models\_from\_hallucinating\_a/](https://www.reddit.com/r/PromptEngineering/comments/1p6krn8/stop_your_diffusion_models_from_hallucinating_a/)  
39. DIFFUSIONRENDERER: Neural Inverse and Forward Rendering with Video Diffusion Models \- CVF Open Access, accessed on April 12, 2026, [https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2025/papers/Liang\_Diffusion\_Renderer\_Neural\_Inverse\_and\_Forward\_Rendering\_with\_Video\_Diffusion\_CVPR\_2025\_paper.pdf](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2025/papers/Liang_Diffusion_Renderer_Neural_Inverse_and_Forward_Rendering_with_Video_Diffusion_CVPR_2025_paper.pdf)
